english: Um (around) + Welt (world). Everything that surrounds you.\nGermans rank environmental protection as a top political priority.
Um (around) + Welt (world). The world around you. Germans rank environmental protection as a top political priority. The Umwelt is not just nature. It is everything that surrounds you.
Umwelt + Schutz (protection). Germany pioneered the Green Party in 1980.\nEmbedded in Article 20a of the German constitution.
Umwelt + Schutz (protection). Germany pioneered the Green Party (Die Grünen) in 1980. Umweltschutz is embedded in Article 20a of the German constitution.
From Greek 'energeia' (activity). The Energiewende aims to\nphase out nuclear and fossil fuels in Germany.
From Greek 'energeia' (activity). 'Erneuerbare Energie' (renewable energy) is Germany's obsession. The Energiewende (energy transition) aims to phase out nuclear and fossil fuels.
From Greek 'klima' (inclination/region). Neuter.\n'Klimawandel' (climate change) dominates German news.
From Greek 'klima' (inclination/region). 'Klimawandel' (climate change) and 'Klimaschutz' (climate protection) dominate German news. 'Klimaneutral' is the target.
Also means 'stream' or 'current.' From the flowing motion.\n'Ökostrom' (green electricity) is a real product you can buy.
'Electricity' AND 'stream/current.' From the flowing motion of electric current. 'Ökostrom' (green electricity) is a real product you can buy. 'Der Strom fließt' works for rivers and wires.
Cognate with English 'loft' (high air). 'Frische Luft' is\na German prescription for everything.
Cognate with English 'loft' (high air). 'Frische Luft' (fresh air) is a German prescription for everything. Feeling tired? 'Geh an die frische Luft!' Air quality is a major environmental metric.
Triple meaning: 'fruchtbarer Boden' (fertile soil),\n'Erdboden' (ground), 'Fußboden' (floor).
Triple meaning. 'Fruchtbarer Boden' (fertile soil), 'Erdboden' (ground), 'Fußboden' (floor). Related to English 'bottom.' The foundation of everything.
Both the planet and the dirt. Cognate with English 'earth.'\n'Die Erde dreht sich' (the earth turns).
Both the planet and the dirt. Cognate with English 'earth.' 'Die Erde dreht sich' (the earth turns). 'In der Erde' (in the soil). Like English, one word for planet and ground.
From Latin 'temperatura.' Germany uses Celsius.\n30+ degrees is a heatwave. Most homes have no AC.
From Latin 'temperatura.' Germany uses Celsius. 0 degrees is freezing, 20 is comfortable, 30+ is a heatwave. Germans panic at 35 degrees because most homes have no air conditioning.
From 'heiß' (hot). 'Hitzewelle' (heatwave).\nGermany's record: 42.6 degrees Celsius in 2019.
From 'heiß' (hot). 'Hitzewelle' (heatwave). Germany's record: 42.6 degrees Celsius in 2019. 'Vor Hitze umkommen' (to perish from heat) is how Germans describe summer.
From French 'contrôle.' 'Qualitätskontrolle' (quality control),\n'Passkontrolle' (passport check).
From French 'contrôle.' 'Qualitätskontrolle' (quality control), 'Passkontrolle' (passport check). Germans love Kontrolle in all forms. Ordnung muss sein.
From Greek 'systema.' Neuter. 'Das Pfandsystem' is world-famous.\nGermany recycles 70%+ of packaging.
From Greek 'systema.' 'Das Pfandsystem' (deposit system) is world-famous. Germany recycles 70%+ of packaging. The system works because it is a System.
From Latin 'statisticum' (of the state). Originally meant state data.\n'Laut Statistik' (according to statistics).
From Latin 'statisticum' (of the state). Originally meant state data. 'Laut Statistik' (according to statistics) is how German presentations begin. Germans trust numbers.
Durch (through) + Schnitt (cut). A cut through the middle.\n'Im Durchschnitt' (on average).
Durch (through) + Schnitt (cut). A cut through the middle. The average. 'Im Durchschnitt' (on average). Literally slicing through data to find the middle. German compound logic at its finest.
Irregular: stieg, gestiegen. 'Die Temperaturen steigen.'\n'Einsteigen' (to board), 'aussteigen' (to exit).
Irregular: stieg, gestiegen. 'Die Temperaturen steigen' (temperatures are rising). 'Einsteigen' (to board), 'aussteigen' (to exit). A verb of upward motion in every context.
Irregular: sank, gesunken. 'Die Kosten sinken.'\nCognate with English 'sink.' The opposite of steigen.
Irregular: sank, gesunken. 'Die Kosten sinken' (costs are falling). Cognate with English 'sink.' The opposite of steigen. These two verbs describe every graph.
From Latin 'reducere.' The -ieren ending marks a borrowed verb.\nRegular conjugation. International and professional.
From Latin 'reducere.' The -ieren ending marks a borrowed verb. 'Emissionen reduzieren' (to reduce emissions). Regular conjugation. International and professional.
From Latin 'extremus.' Functions as both adjective and\nintensifying adverb. 'Extrem wichtig' (extremely important).
From Latin 'extremus.' 'Extreme Hitze' (extreme heat), 'extrem wichtig' (extremely important). Functions as both adjective and intensifying adverb. Germans use it like English 'extremely.'
Ab (off/away) + Fall (fall). Something that falls away.\n'Abfall trennen' (to separate waste) is a German commandment.
Ab (off/away) + Fall (fall). Something that falls away, waste. 'Abfall trennen' (to separate waste) is a German commandment. Every German household sorts into at least four bins.
Abfall + Eimer (bucket). Germans have color-coded bins:\nyellow for packaging, blue for paper, brown for organic.
Abfall + Eimer (bucket). Waste bucket. Germans have color-coded Abfalleimer: yellow for packaging, blue for paper, brown for organic. Putting wrong items in the wrong bin is a social crime.
Müll (trash) + Abfuhr (removal). In Germany, you pay\nper kilogram of trash. Less waste means lower bills.
Müll (trash) + Abfuhr (removal). 'Die Müllabfuhr kommt montags' (The garbage truck comes on Mondays). In Germany, you pay per kilogram of trash. Less waste means lower bills.
Müll + Tonne (barrel/bin). Every German address has\nMülltonnen in four colors. Wrong sorting is a social crime.
Müll + Tonne (barrel/bin). Every German address has Mülltonnen in four colors. Putting the wrong item in the wrong Tonne is a social crime that neighbors will correct.
From Greek 'plastikos' (moldable). Neuter.\nGermany banned single-use plastic bags in 2022.
From Greek 'plastikos' (moldable). Germany banned single-use plastic bags in 2022. 'Plastikfrei' (plastic-free) is a lifestyle movement. The Pfand system tackles plastic bottles.
Kunst (art/artificial) + Stoff (material). The formal word\nfor plastic. Preferred in industry and science.

Kunst (art/artificial) + Stoff (material). Literally 'artificial material.' The formal German word for plastic. 'Kunststoffverpackung' (plastic packaging). More technical than Plastik, preferred in industry.
Cognate with English 'holt' (a small wood). 'Aus Holz' (made of wood).\nThe Black Forest is 60% of Baden-Württemberg's area.
Cognate with English 'holt' (a small wood). 'Aus Holz' (made of wood). Germany's Black Forest (Schwarzwald) covers 60% of Baden-Württemberg. 'Holz hacken' (to chop wood) is a German weekend activity.
From Greek 'metallon' (mine/quarry). Neuter.\nGermany recycles 93% of metal packaging.
From Greek 'metallon' (mine/quarry). Neuter. 'Altmetall' (scrap metal). Germany recycles 93% of metal packaging. 'Schwermetall' means both heavy metal (chemistry) and heavy metal (music).
From Latin 'materialis.' Neuter. Plural: Materialien.\n'Baumaterial' (building material), 'Rohmaterial' (raw material).
From Latin 'materialis.' Neuter. 'Baumaterial' (building material), 'Rohmaterial' (raw material). International word, same in dozens of languages. Plural: Materialien.
Triple meaning: 'Rohstoff' (raw material), 'Baumwollstoff'\n(cotton fabric), 'Unterrichtsstoff' (lesson material).
Triple meaning. 'Rohstoff' (raw material), 'Werkstoff' (working material), 'Baumwollstoff' (cotton fabric). Also: 'Unterrichtsstoff' (lesson material). Anything that constitutes something.
'Waren' (goods, plural). 'Warenhaus' (department store).\nRelated to English 'ware' (as in hardware, silverware).
'Waren' (goods, plural). 'Hardware' and 'Software' were borrowed back from English. 'Warenhaus' (department store). Related to English 'ware' (as in hardware, silverware).
From 'handeln' (to trade/act). 'Einzelhandel' (retail),\n'Großhandel' (wholesale), 'Außenhandel' (foreign trade).
From 'handeln' (to trade/act). 'Einzelhandel' (retail), 'Großhandel' (wholesale), 'Außenhandel' (foreign trade). 'Fair Handel' (fair trade) is a growing movement.
From Handel. 'Autohändler' (car dealer),\n'Buchhändler' (bookseller). The -er suffix creates the agent.
From Handel. 'Autohändler' (car dealer), 'Buchhändler' (bookseller). The -er suffix creates the person who does the action. A Händler handles Handel.
The feminine form. Händler + -in.\nGerman consistently creates feminine forms with -in.
The feminine form. Händler + -in. 'Die Händlerin auf dem Markt' (the female trader at the market). German consistently creates feminine forms with -in for person nouns.
From Latin 'exportare' (to carry out). 'Made in Germany'\nis the world's most trusted export label.
From Latin 'exportare' (to carry out). 'Made in Germany' is the world's most trusted export label. Germany is the third-largest exporter globally. Cars, machines, chemicals.
From Latin 'importare' (to carry in). The opposite of Export.\nGermany imports energy, raw materials, and coffee.
From Latin 'importare' (to carry in). The opposite of Export. 'Importware' (imported goods). Germany imports energy, raw materials, and coffee. Germans are Europe's biggest coffee drinkers.
From Latin 'industria' (diligence). 'Schwerindustrie'\n(heavy industry). 'Industrie 4.0' is Germany's digital strategy.
From Latin 'industria' (diligence). 'Schwerindustrie' (heavy industry). Germany's Ruhrgebiet was Europe's industrial heart. 'Industrie 4.0' is Germany's digital manufacturing strategy.
From Latin 'productio.' The -tion ending is always feminine\nin German and always stressed on the last syllable.
From Latin 'productio.' 'Massenproduktion' (mass production). 'Die Produktion einstellen' (to stop production). The -tion ending is always feminine in German and always stressed on the last syllable.
The -ieren verb from Produktion. Regular conjugation.\n'In Deutschland produziert' (produced in Germany).
The -ieren verb from Produktion. Regular conjugation. 'In Deutschland produziert' (produced in Germany). 'Überproduzieren' (to overproduce). Reliable borrowed verb.
From Latin 'alternare.' 'Alternative Energien' (alternative energies).\n'Die Alternative' (noun) means the alternative option.
From Latin 'alternare.' 'Alternative Energien' (alternative energies), 'alternative Medizin' (alternative medicine). 'Die Alternative' (the noun) means the alternative option.
For packaging waste (Verpackung): plastic, aluminum, tetra paks.\nPart of Germany's Duales System (recycling system).

Germany's color-coded bin system: Gelbe Tonne (packaging), Blaue Tonne (paper), Braune Tonne (organic), Schwarze Tonne (residual waste). Getting it wrong can earn you a stern lecture from your neighbors.
Ab (away) + Gas. The gas that goes away from the engine.\n'Abgasnormen' (emission standards) are strict in Germany.
Ab (away) + Gas. Exhaust gas. 'Autoabgase' (car exhaust fumes). The word says what it is: gas that goes away from the engine. 'Abgasnormen' (emission standards) are strict in Germany.
'In Gefahr' (in danger). 'Lebensgefahr' (mortal danger).\nFrom Old High German 'fara' (ambush).
From Old High German 'fara' (ambush). 'In Gefahr' (in danger). 'Lebensgefahr' (mortal danger). 'Gefährlich' (dangerous). Danger originally meant an unexpected attack.
From Greek 'katastrophe' (overturning). Germans say\n'Das ist eine Katastrophe!' for any major problem.

From Greek 'katastrophe' (overturning). 'Naturkatastrophe' (natural disaster), 'Umweltkatastrophe' (environmental disaster). Germans say 'Das ist eine Katastrophe!' for any major problem, from floods to late trains.
From Greek 'krisis' (decision/turning point). A Krise is\na turning point, not necessarily the end.
From Greek 'krisis' (decision/turning point). 'Klimakrise' (climate crisis), 'Wirtschaftskrise' (economic crisis). A Krise is a turning point, not necessarily the end. It demands a decision.
From 'schützen' (to protect). 'Umweltschutz,' 'Datenschutz,'\n'Mutterschutz.' Germans protect everything with a compound.
From 'schützen' (to protect). 'Umweltschutz' (environmental protection), 'Datenschutz' (data protection), 'Mutterschutz' (maternity protection). Germans protect everything with a compound.
From 'sicher' (safe/certain). The -heit suffix creates abstract nouns.\n'Verkehrssicherheit' (traffic safety).
From 'sicher' (safe/certain). 'Verkehrssicherheit' (traffic safety), 'Lebensmittelsicherheit' (food safety). The -heit suffix creates abstract nouns. Sicher + heit = the state of being safe.
From 'forschen' (to research). Germany spends 3%+ of GDP\non Forschung. The Fraunhofer institutes are world-famous.
From 'forschen' (to research). 'Klimaforschung' (climate research), 'Grundlagenforschung' (basic research). Germany spends 3%+ of GDP on Forschung. The Fraunhofer institutes are world-famous.
From 'fordern' (to demand). Not to be confused with 'fördern'\n(to promote/support). One Umlaut changes everything.
From 'fordern' (to demand). Not to be confused with 'fördern' (to promote/support). One Umlaut changes demand into support. 'Die Forderung nach Klimaschutz' (the demand for climate protection).
Um (around) + Frage (question). Asking around.\n'Laut einer Umfrage' (according to a survey).
Um (around) + Frage (question). Asking around. A survey or poll. 'Laut einer Umfrage' (according to a survey). Germans love Umfragen. Every news broadcast cites one.
'Die Umwelt schützen' (to protect the environment).\n'Sich schützen' (to protect oneself). Regular verb.
'Die Umwelt schützen' (to protect the environment). 'Sich schützen' (to protect oneself). Regular verb. The ü-Umlaut gives it its distinctive German sound.
Cognate with English 'warn.' 'Vor Gefahr warnen'\n(to warn of danger). Regular verb.
Cognate with English 'warn.' 'Vor Gefahr warnen' (to warn of danger). 'Unwetterwarnung' (severe weather warning). Regular verb. The Wetter-App sends Warnungen constantly.
No Umlaut. Different from 'fördern' (to support).\n'Wir fordern Klimaschutz' (We demand climate protection).
Different from 'fördern' (to support). No Umlaut here. 'Wir fordern Klimaschutz' (We demand climate protection). Regular verb. The missing dots change the meaning completely.
Irregular: vermied, vermieden. 'Plastik vermeiden' (to avoid plastic).\nThe ver- prefix intensifies 'meiden' (to shun).
Irregular: vermied, vermieden. 'Plastik vermeiden' (to avoid plastic). 'Fehler vermeiden' (to avoid mistakes). The ver- prefix intensifies 'meiden' (to shun).
Ver- + Ursache (cause). 'Abgase verursachen Luftverschmutzung.'\nThe compound logic: to cause = to make into a cause.
Ver- + Ursache (cause). 'Abgase verursachen Luftverschmutzung' (Exhaust fumes cause air pollution). Regular verb. The compound logic: to cause = to make into a cause.
Ver- + Schmutz (dirt). 'Die Luft verschmutzen' (to pollute the air).\nVer- often means 'to make into' something.
Ver- + Schmutz (dirt). 'Die Luft verschmutzen' (to pollute the air). 'Umweltverschmutzung' (environmental pollution). Ver- often means 'to make into' something.
From 'Gift' (poison). False friend: English 'gift' = present.\nGerman 'Gift' = poison. Nobody's present.
From 'Gift' (poison). 'Giftige Chemikalien' (toxic chemicals). Fun false friend: English 'gift' = present. German 'Gift' = poison. 'Giftmüll' (toxic waste) is nobody's present.
From Greek 'oikos' (house) + 'logos' (study). Often shortened to\n'öko-' as prefix: 'Ökostrom' (green electricity).
From Greek 'oikos' (house) + 'logos' (study). 'Ökologischer Landbau' (organic farming). Often shortened to 'öko-' as a prefix: 'Ökostrom' (green electricity).
From 'sparen' (to save). Germans consider Sparsamkeit\n(thriftiness) a virtue. Being sparsam is a compliment.
From 'sparen' (to save). 'Sparsam mit Wasser umgehen' (to use water sparingly). Germans consider Sparsamkeit (thriftiness) a virtue. Being sparsam is a compliment.

German can turn any verb into an adjective describing an ongoing action. Formation: infinitive + d.

steigen (to rise) becomes steigend (rising)
lachen (to laugh) becomes lachend (laughing)
wachsen (to grow) becomes wachsend (growing)

Then it declines like a normal adjective:
- der steigende Preis (the rising price)
- die wachsende Gefahr (the growing danger)
- ein sinkendes Schiff (a sinking ship)

Like English: 'the laughing man' = 'der lachende Mann.' Same logic, same word order. Partizip I always describes an ongoing action.

The Partizip I is formed by adding -d to the infinitive. It always describes an ongoing action happening at the same time as the main verb.

Important: The Partizip I is used primarily as an adjective in German. Unlike English, you cannot use it to form progressive tenses ('I am running'). German says 'Ich laufe' (I run/am running).

As an adjective, it declines normally:
- der lachende Mann (nominative masculine)
- die lachenden Männer (nominative plural)
- mit steigendem Interesse (dative neuter)

As an adverb, it has no ending: 'Er ging lachend weg' (He left laughing).
From Latin 'fabrica' (workshop). Many old Fabriken are\nnow museums or cultural centers in the Ruhrgebiet.
From Latin 'fabrica' (workshop). 'Chemiefabrik' (chemical factory), 'Autofabrik' (car factory). The Ruhrgebiet had thousands. Many are now museums or cultural centers.
Kraft (power) + Werk (work/plant). Germany is shutting down\nnuclear Kraftwerke and building wind and solar ones.
Kraft (power) + Werk (work/plant). 'Atomkraftwerk' (nuclear power plant), 'Kohlekraftwerk' (coal power plant). Germany is shutting down nuclear ones.
From 'anlegen' (to lay out). 'Solaranlage' (solar installation),\n'Kläranlage' (water treatment plant). Context determines meaning.
From 'anlegen' (to lay out). 'Solaranlage' (solar installation), 'Kläranlage' (water treatment plant), 'Geldanlage' (financial investment). Context determines which meaning.
From 'werben' (to advertise/court). 'Keine Werbung!'\non German mailboxes means no junk mail.
From 'werben' (to advertise/court). 'Werbung machen' (to advertise). 'Keine Werbung!' on German mailboxes means no junk mail. Werbung is both the industry and the ads.
From French 'réclame.' Slightly old-fashioned or pejorative.\nUsed for flashy or aggressive ads.
From French 'réclame.' Advertising, but slightly old-fashioned or pejorative. 'Reklame machen' sounds less professional than 'Werbung machen.' Used for flashy or aggressive ads.
'Briefmarke' (postage stamp), 'Automarke' (car brand).\n'Deutsche Marken' are globally trusted.
'Briefmarke' (postage stamp), 'Automarke' (car brand). 'Deutsche Marken' are globally trusted. The word comes from 'markieren' (to mark). A brand marks its products.
From Italian 'modello.' Neuter. 'Geschäftsmodell' (business model),\n'Automodell' (car model).
From Italian 'modello.' Neuter. 'Geschäftsmodell' (business model), 'Automodell' (car model). 'Ein Modell für Nachhaltigkeit' (a model for sustainability).
From Latin 'factor' (maker). 'Ein wichtiger Faktor.'\nInternational word, same in most European languages.
From Latin 'factor' (maker). 'Ein wichtiger Faktor' (an important factor). 'Risikofaktor' (risk factor). International word, same in most European languages.
'Vorstufe' (preliminary stage), 'Entwicklungsstufe'\n(development stage). Also literally a stair step.
'Vorstufe' (preliminary stage), 'Entwicklungsstufe' (development stage). Also literally a stair step. 'Vorsicht, Stufe!' (Watch out, step!) is on signs everywhere.
Cognate with English 'net.' 'Stromnetz' (power grid),\n'Ins Netz gehen' means to go online or to fall into a trap.
Cognate with English 'net.' 'Internet' (internet), 'Stromnetz' (power grid). 'Ins Netz gehen' (to go into the net) means to go online. Also: to fall into a trap.
Netz + Werk (work). 'Soziales Netzwerk' (social network).\nGermans turned 'Netzwerken' into a verb for professional networking.
Netz + Werk (work). 'Soziales Netzwerk' (social network). 'Ein Netzwerk aufbauen' (to build a network). Professional networking is 'Netzwerken.' Germans turned it into a verb.
From Greek 'techne' (art/skill). Emphasizes practical application.\n'Informationstechnik' (IT).
From Greek 'techne' (art/skill). 'Spitzentechnik' (cutting-edge technology). 'Informationstechnik' (IT). 'Technik' emphasizes practical application, 'Technologie' the science.
Technik + -logie (study of). More academic than Technik.\nThe -ie ending is always stressed.
Technik + -logie (study of). 'Umwelttechnologie' (environmental technology), 'Biotechnologie' (biotechnology). More academic and theoretical than Technik. The -ie ending is always stressed.
Er- + hoch (high). Transitive: someone raises something.\nCompare: steigen (intransitive, something rises on its own).
Er- + hoch (high). 'Die Temperatur erhöhen' (to increase the temperature). Transitive: someone raises something. Compare: steigen (intransitive, something rises on its own).
WITH Umlaut, unlike fordern (to demand). 'Erneuerbare Energien\nfördern.' 'Förderung' (funding/support).
WITH Umlaut, unlike fordern (to demand). 'Erneuerbare Energien fördern' (to promote renewable energies). 'Förderung' (funding/support). One dot changes demand to support.
From Latin 'protestari.' Regular -ieren verb.\n'Fridays for Future' made Germany a global protest capital.
From Latin 'protestari.' Regular -ieren verb. 'Gegen den Klimawandel protestieren' (to protest against climate change). 'Fridays for Future' started protests worldwide, including Germany.
From Statistik. 'Statistisch gesehen' (statistically speaking).\nThe -isch ending creates adjectives from nouns.
From Statistik. 'Statistisch gesehen' (statistically speaking). The -isch ending creates adjectives from nouns. 'Statistisch signifikant' (statistically significant).
From Durchschnitt. The -lich ending on compound nouns.\n'Durchschnittlich 20 Grad' (an average of 20 degrees).
From Durchschnitt. 'Durchschnittlich 20 Grad' (an average of 20 degrees). The -lich ending on compound nouns: Durchschnitt + lich = on average. Long but logical.

You know the Partizip II from the Perfekt: 'Ich habe das Buch gelesen.' Now as an adjective:

gelesen (read) becomes: der gelesene Brief (the read letter)
recycelt (recycled) becomes: das recycelte Papier (the recycled paper)
verschmutzt (polluted) becomes: die verschmutzte Luft (the polluted air)

Declension like any adjective. The key difference from Partizip I:
- Partizip I (ongoing): der lesende Mann (the man who IS reading)
- Partizip II (completed): der gelesene Brief (the letter that HAS BEEN read)

Like English: 'the broken window,' 'the recycled paper.'
